Sealed-bid auction with three bidders. Auctioneer opens to each
bidder sequentially; each replies with a numeric bid; Auctioneer
computes winner and announces 'winner' or 'outbid' to each.
Refinements: bids positive; winner announcement uses one of the
reserved keywords. v1 is single-round (no recursion); v2 will add
the bidding-loop pattern.

Clinical-trial patient enrolment. Coordinator screens with PI;
if eligible, takes Patient consent + Lab baseline (sequenced) and
obtains EthicsBoard approval; then enrols. Refinements: eligibility
verdict explicit; baseline values in physiological range; ethics
approval explicit.

Multi-source retrieval-augmented generation with a bounded verification
loop. Tests recursion and content correctness — two retrievers fetch in
parallel, the synthesizer drafts an answer, the fact-checker verifies it,
and on failure the synthesizer revises and re-submits until verified or a
documented "cannot answer" is emitted.

Shape F — loop + complicated (nested) branching. Editorial workflow with
per-round revision; outer choice is revise-vs-accept, and each side has
its own inner choice (major-vs-minor edit on revise; publish-vs-schedule
on accept). Exercises shape F: rec + nested choice.

DEADLOCK / SAGA-ORDERING CASE, sourced from real agent skills. Based on the
LangGraph supervisor + booking-saga pattern (langchain-ai/langgraph): a
traveler orchestrator coordinates a hotel-reservation service and a payment
service to book a stay, with saga-style ordering between reserve and pay.

Each service's skill is individually sensible but they deadlock together: the
Hotel service's skill says "don't confirm the room until payment is secured",
and the Payment service's skill says "don't charge until the room is
confirmed". Each waits for the other -> circular wait -> the booking never
completes (the saga hangs). No human reading either skill alone flags it; a
formal check catches the cycle and forces a reserve-first ordering (hold the
room, then charge, then confirm) that breaks it.

SAFETY / EXECUTE-BEFORE-REVIEW CASE, sourced from real agent skills. Based on
the AutoGen two-agent code pattern (microsoft/autogen): an assistant that
writes code and a code-executor that runs it. In the common configuration the
executor runs whatever code it receives automatically (human_input_mode set to
never / auto-execute), with no human or reviewer gate in between.

Read in isolation the skills let the Coder hand code to the Executor and the
Executor run it immediately — before any Reviewer has vetted it. The required
order is Code -> Reviewer Approval -> Execute; the skills do not encode the
Approval gate, so unreviewed (possibly destructive) code executes. This is the
"execute before review" disaster class.

Shape E — loop + simple branching. Worker attempts a task; Manager
inspects the result each round and either accepts (terminating the loop,
with Auditor confirmation) or rejects and loops back for another attempt.
Each branch carries substantive payload work, unlike shape D.
Scale task: 10-role linear pipeline. Same shape as report_pipeline but larger, to show global-text token cost grows with protocol size while the projected local contract stays flat.

THE DEADLOCK DEMO. A goods-for-payment trade whose plausible, human-written
per-agent skills contain a CIRCULAR WAIT: the Buyer's skill says "don't pay
until the goods arrive", the Seller's skill says "don't ship until paid". Each
waits for the other -> runtime deadlock -> agents burn tokens replying WAIT
across every retry -> 0% completion. No human or LLM reading those two skills
in isolation flags the problem; Scribble's deadlock check catches it instantly
and forces the Escrow-first fix that breaks the cycle. This case exists to
prove: (1) unchecked specs/skills can deadlock; (2) the deadlock is costly;
(3) a formal checker prevents it for free (at design time).

Travel booking with three suppliers (Hotel, Flight, Car). Customer
asks the Agent to book; the Agent sequences three reservations; on
success the Agent charges and confirms. Refinements: each
confirmation carries a positive price; total charge within plausible
bounds. v1 is the happy path only — rollback/compensation in v2.
Coordinate Src, W0 and Sink through a multi-step exchange that starts with Task0 and ends with Report.

Travel booking with all-or-nothing rollback. Tests transactional integrity
with exception propagation across roles — flight, hotel, and car are quoted,
then either all are confirmed and payment is charged, or any failure triggers
a clean rollback (all bookings cancelled, no payment charged).

Cross-party goods-for-payment settlement. The user's stated intent contains a
HIDDEN CIRCULAR DEPENDENCY (a deadlock): the Buyer will only pay after the
goods ship, and the Seller will only ship after payment — each waits for the
other. A human (or an LLM) reading the intent rarely notices; Scribble's
deadlock check catches it immediately. A safe protocol must break the cycle
with the Escrow holding funds first. This case tests: (a) does the validator
catch a deadlock the intent hides, and (b) once given a valid protocol, do
agents reach settlement — with global text vs projected local contracts, and
with vs without enforcement.

Finance reporting with 2x2 nested choice. Outer choice at Fetcher
(high vs standard revenue) and a different inner chooser per outer arm
(TaxSpecialist for high, RevenueAnalyst for standard). Each inner
chooser must read the payload from the outer step and pick the right
sub-branch. This is the complex-branching variant (v1c) of the finance
domain — designed to expose Finding 2 (LLM picks wrong message label)
at choice points where the cue lives in payload, not label.

invariants under a value-dependent choice and an exception (rejection)
path — large transfers must route through an Approver before any money
moves; small transfers skip approval; insufficient funds reject cleanly.

WRONG-ORDER SAFETY CASE, sourced from real agent skills. Based on the
OpenAI Agents SDK "customer_service" airline example
(openai/openai-agents-python, examples/customer_service/main.py). The real
Seat Booking agent's instructions are: "1. Ask for confirmation number.
2. Ask desired seat. 3. Use the update seat tool." — with NO mention that a
flight must first be assigned. In the real code the flight is only assigned
by the Triage -> Seat-Booking HANDOFF HOOK (on_seat_booking_handoff sets
flight_number), and update_seat() has `assert flight_number is not None`.

So the prose skills, read in isolation, let the Seat-Booking agent call
UpdateSeat BEFORE the flight has been assigned by Triage -> the seat is
written against a null/unknown flight (or the assert blows up in prod). The
required order is AssignFlight (Triage) THEN UpdateSeat (Seat-Booking), but
nothing in the skills encodes it. This is the "do A before B, but the skill
goes straight to B" disaster class.

Finance reporting with 2x2 nested choice. Outer choice at Fetcher
(high vs standard revenue) and a different inner chooser per outer arm
(TaxSpecialist for high, RevenueAnalyst for standard). Each inner
chooser must read the payload from the outer step and pick the right
sub-branch. This is the complex-branching variant (v1c) of the finance
domain — designed to expose Finding 2 (LLM picks wrong message label)
at choice points where the cue lives in payload, not label.
Coordinate Hub, S0, S1, S2 and S3 through a multi-step exchange that starts with Ask0 and ends with Ans3.
